name: Gorman, Jamie C.
recent_1: Zhou, S., Duan, W., Yin, X., Scalia, M., Hao, R., Weng, N., Funke, G., Tolston, M., Freeman, G., Schelble, B., Gorman, J. C., & McNeese, N. (2026). The spread of trust and distrust in human-AI teams. Applied Ergonomics, 130, 104648. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2025.104648
recent_1_synopsis: This study examines how trust and distrust spread among human and AI teammates within human-AI teams, investigating how these dynamics affect team performance outcomes. Findings reveal that mismatches between communicative and behavioral trust-distrust spreading can paradoxically improve performance, and that team composition (human-majority vs. AI-majority) moderates how trust and distrust contagion affect team outcomes. The work advances understanding of interpersonal trust dynamics in increasingly common hybrid human-AI work configurations.
recent_2: Duan, W., Flathmann, C., McNeese, N., Scalia, M. J., Zhang, R., Gorman, J., Freeman, G., Zhou, S., Hauptman, A. I., & Yin, X. (2025). Trusting autonomous teammates in human-AI teams - A literature review. Proceedings of the 2025 CHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems, pp. 1-23. DOI: 10.1145/3706598.3713527
recent_2_synopsis: This systematic literature review synthesizes research on how humans develop, calibrate, and maintain trust in autonomous AI teammates within human-AI team contexts. The review maps the antecedents, dynamics, and consequences of trust across varied human-AI team configurations, identifying key gaps in current empirical and theoretical understanding. It provides a comprehensive framework that positions trust calibration as central to effective human-autonomy teaming.
recent_3: Gorman, J. C. (2024). Simultaneous hypotheses in cognitive agents: Commentary on Paxton, Necaise et al., and the dynamical hypothesis in cognitive science. Topics in Cognitive Science. DOI: 10.1111/tops.12772
recent_3_synopsis: This theoretical commentary engages with the dynamical hypothesis in cognitive science, arguing that cognitive agents—including human teams—can operate under simultaneous, competing hypotheses rather than sequentially resolving uncertainty. Gorman applies insights from team coordination dynamics and enaction theory to extend the dynamical systems framework to team-level cognition. The piece situates interaction-dominant dynamics within broader debates about the nature of cognitive representation.
seminal_1: Cooke, N. J., Gorman, J. C., Myers, C. W., & Duran, J. L. (2013). Interactive team cognition. Cognitive Science, 37(2), 255-285. DOI: 10.1111/cogs.12009
seminal_1_synopsis: This highly influential paper (~975 citations as of June 2026, Google Scholar) introduces Interactive Team Cognition (ITC) theory, arguing that team cognition is fundamentally an activity rather than a static property or product, that it must be measured at the team level, and that it is inseparable from the task context in which it occurs. ITC challenged prevailing mental-model-based approaches by locating cognition in the dynamic interactions between teammates. The framework has become a foundational reference for researchers studying team coordination, distributed cognition, and human-autonomy teaming.
seminal_2: Gorman, J. C., Cooke, N. J., & Amazeen, P. G. (2010). Training adaptive teams. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 52(2), 295-307. DOI: 10.1177/0018720810371689
seminal_2_synopsis: This paper (~284 citations as of June 2026, Google Scholar) reports an experiment comparing three training approaches—cross-training, perturbation training, and procedural training—for building team adaptability in a simulated uninhabited air vehicle task with collaborators Nancy Cooke and Polemnia Amazeen. Perturbation training, which intentionally disrupts coordination patterns during acquisition, produced superior adaptation to novel situations compared to established methods. The study introduced the perturbation-training paradigm that has since influenced both team-training practice and the theoretical understanding of team coordination dynamics as a malleable, nonlinear system.
expertise_overview: Jamie C. Gorman is a Professor of Human Systems Engineering and Director of the Center for Human, Artificial Intelligence, and Robot Teaming (CHART) at Arizona State University. His research applies dynamical systems theory and nonlinear methods to the measurement and modeling of team coordination, with a particular focus on how real-time interaction patterns predict team performance and adaptability. A central thread of his work is the quantification of team coordination dynamics using entropy, recurrence quantification analysis, and related time-series techniques applied to communication, control, and behavioral data streams. Gorman has extended this framework from all-human teams to human-autonomy teaming (HAT), studying how synthetic agents affect coordination flexibility, trust calibration, and collective performance under degraded conditions. His domain contexts include military command-and-control, remotely piloted aircraft systems, space missions, medical teams, and educational settings. He collaborates extensively with Nancy Cooke, Polemnia Amazeen, and colleagues in the CHART network on both foundational theory and applied training interventions.
notes: High confidence identification. Confirmed via Google Scholar profile (user=CQxNrHAAAAAJ), ASU faculty/news pages (news.asu.edu, asu.elsevierpure.com), and ASU Systems Psychology Lab publication list (labs.engineering.asu.edu/gorman). Gorman moved from Georgia Tech to Arizona State University in Fall 2022; he is currently Professor of Human Systems Engineering and Director of CHART at ASU Polytechnic School. Citation counts sourced from Google Scholar (accessed June 2026): Interactive Team Cognition ~975 citations; Training Adaptive Teams ~284 citations; Dynamic Measures of Team Adaptation ~15 citations (pre-verified HF paper). All DOIs verified via Crossref API (June 2026): 10.1016/j.apergo.2025.104648 (spread of trust), 10.1145/3706598.3713527 (CHI 2025 lit review), 10.1111/tops.12772 (Topics in Cognitive Science 2024 commentary), 10.1111/cogs.12009 (Interactive Team Cognition), 10.1177/0018720810371689 (Training Adaptive Teams). Pre-verified HF seed DOIs also confirmed real. No unverifiable citations.
